modules.title: What is a Set?
modules.hook: A WhatsApp group is a 'set' only if there's no doubt who's in it. That's the whole idea.
modules.definition: A set is a well-defined collection of objects. 'Well-defined' means: given any object, you can say for certain whether it belongs or not — no opinions allowed.
notation: Sets: capital letters — $A, B, C, X, Y, Z$
Elements: small letters — $a, b, c, x, y, z$

concepts.heading: Two ways to write a set
concepts.body:
**Roster form**: list every element, comma-separated, inside braces. Order doesn't matter, repeats aren't written twice.
$$\{2,4,6\}$$
**Set-builder form**: describe the common property.
$$\{x : x \text{ is an even natural number less than } 7\}$$

video.caption: Coming soon: What makes a collection 'well-defined'

tricks: If the description uses a subjective word — 'best', 'most talented', 'renowned' — it is NOT a set.
In roster form, order and repetition don't matter: $\{1,2,3\} = \{3,2,1\} = \{2,2,1,3,3\}$.

examples.question: Write $\{x : x^2 + x - 2 = 0\}$ in roster form.
Write $\{1,4,9,16,25,\dots\}$ in set-builder form.
steps: Each term is a perfect square of a natural number

quiz.q: Which of these IS a well-defined set?
options: The collection of ten most talented writers of India
The collection of all prime numbers less than 20
A team of eleven best cricket batsmen of the world
A collection of most dangerous animals of the world

feedback.0: 'Most talented' is subjective — different people would list different writers. Not well-defined.
feedback.2: 'Best' is a matter of opinion. Not well-defined.
feedback.3: 'Most dangerous' is subjective. Not well-defined.

quiz.q: Roster form of $\{x : x \text{ is a natural number}, 3 < x < 10\}$?

feedback.0: You included 3 and 10 — but the inequalities are strict ($<$), so both endpoints are excluded.
feedback.2: 10 is excluded because $x < 10$ is strict.
feedback.3: 3 is excluded because $x > 3$ is strict.

options: $\{x : x \text{ is an odd natural number}\}$
$\{x : x \text{ is an even natural number}\}$

feedback.0: This misses the 'even' condition — it would include 1,2,3...
feedback.1: Odd numbers are 1,3,5 — the wrong parity.
feedback.3: This describes only the single number 2, not the whole set.

quiz.q: Roster form of the set of prime factors of 30?

feedback.0: That's ALL divisors of 30, not just prime ones. 1, 6, 10, 15, 30 aren't prime.
feedback.2: 15 is not prime (it's $3\times5$).
feedback.3: 6 is not prime and 3 is missing.

feedback.0: 'b' is a consonant, not in V.
feedback.1: 'a' IS a vowel, so $a \in V$, not $\notin$.
feedback.3: V has 5 elements: a, e, i, o, u.

quiz.q: The set of letters in the word 'SCHOOL' in roster form is:

feedback.0: 'O' is repeated in SCHOOL but a set never repeats elements — write it once.
feedback.2: You dropped 'O' entirely — it must appear once.
feedback.3: 'L' shouldn't be repeated either.

modules.title: Types of Sets
modules.hook: Think of an empty WhatsApp group (0 members) vs. a group whose only member is another empty group (weird, but that's 1 member) — that's exactly the $\varnothing$ vs. $\{\varnothing\}$ trap, the most common one in this chapter.
modules.definition: **Empty set** ($\varnothing$ or $\{\}$): has no elements. **Finite set**: empty or has a countable, definite number of elements. **Infinite set**: not finite. **Singleton set**: has exactly one element. **Equal sets**: $A = B$ if every element of $A$ is in $B$ and vice versa.

concepts.heading: The φ vs {0} vs {φ} trap
concepts.body: $\varnothing$ has **0 elements**. $\{0\}$ has **1 element** — the number zero. $\{\varnothing\}$ also has **1 element** — the empty set itself, sitting inside another set. None of these three are the same object.

video.caption: Coming soon: φ vs {0} vs {φ} explained visually

tricks: $\varnothing$ is considered FINITE (it has a definite number of elements: zero).
To check if two sets are equal, check both directions: every element of A in B, AND every element of B in A.

examples.question: Is $\{x : x \in \mathbb{N}, 1 < x < 2\}$ empty?
steps: There is no natural number strictly between 1 and 2

examples.question: Are $A=\{1,2,3,4\}$ and $B=\{3,1,4,2\}$ equal?
steps: Every element of A is in B
Every element of B is in A

feedback.0: $\{\varnothing\}$ is a set CONTAINING the empty set — that's one element, not zero.
feedback.2: Only one object sits inside the braces: $\varnothing$ itself.

quiz.q: Which of these is the empty set?

feedback.0: This has one element (zero) — not empty.
feedback.2: This has one element (the empty set itself) — not empty.
feedback.3: Every $x$ equals itself, so this is a huge (infinite) set, not empty.

quiz.q: $\{x : x \text{ is an even prime number} > 2\}$ is:
options: Empty, since 2 is the only even prime

feedback.1: The condition says GREATER than 2 — 2 itself is excluded.
feedback.2: There are no elements at all satisfying this — it's empty, not infinite.
feedback.3: None of these are prime.

quiz.q: Is the empty set finite or infinite?

feedback.1: It has a definite (zero) count of elements — that makes it finite by definition.
feedback.2: Every set is classified as either finite or infinite; empty set falls under finite.

quiz.q: $A = \{n : n \in \mathbb{Z}, n^2 \le 4\}$ and $B = \{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$. Are A and B equal?

feedback.1: List A: $n^2 \le 4$ gives exactly $-2,-1,0,1,2$ — same as B.
feedback.2: Both sets have exactly the same 5 elements.

feedback.1: $x^2=4$ gives $x=2$ or $x=-2$ — two elements, not one.
feedback.2: Empty set has zero elements, not one.
feedback.3: This has two elements.

modules.title: Subsets & Intervals
modules.hook: Being IN a friend group ($\in$) is different from your 'Close Friends' list being a smaller group INSIDE your followers ($\subset$) — mixing these two up is where every mark gets lost in this topic.
modules.definition: $A$ is a subset of $B$ (written $A \subset B$) if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$. If additionally $A \ne B$, $A$ is a **proper subset**. $\varnothing$ is a subset of every set. Every set is a subset of itself.
notation: $A \subset B$ — A is a subset of B
$A \not\subset B$ — A is not a subset of B

concepts.heading: ∈ vs ⊂ — the core distinction
concepts.body: $a \in A$ means $a$ is a *member* of $A$. $\{a\} \subset A$ means the SET containing $a$ is *contained* in $A$. An element is never a subset of itself in that raw form — $\{a\}$ is the subset, not $a$ alone.

concepts.heading: Counting subsets
concepts.body: A set with $n$ elements has exactly $2^n$ subsets (including $\varnothing$ and the set itself).

video.caption: Coming soon: ∈ vs ⊂ explained with a Venn zoom-in

tricks: $\varnothing \subset A$ is ALWAYS true, for any set A — write it as your first step in subset questions.
Subsets of a set with $n$ elements = $2^n$. For $\{1,2,3\}$, that's $2^3=8$.
$[a,b)$ excludes $b$; $(a,b]$ excludes $a$ — the bracket shape tells you which side is 'closed' (included).

examples.question: Write $\{x : x \in \mathbb{R}, -5 < x \le 7\}$ as an interval.

options: Yes, $\{3,4\}$ is an element of A, and elements can be subsets too
No, $\{3,4\}$ is an ELEMENT of A, not a subset — but $\{\{3,4\}\} \subset A$ is true

feedback.0: $\{3,4\}$ sits inside A as a single element — being AN element doesn't make it a subset unless every element of $\{3,4\}$ (i.e. 3 and 4) is separately in A, which they aren't.
feedback.2: There's one correct answer here — check whether 3 and 4 individually belong to A.

feedback.0: Use $2^n$: $n=2$ elements gives $2^2=4$, not 2.
feedback.1: You likely forgot $\varnothing$ or the full set itself — both count.
feedback.3: Even a 2-element set has more than 1 subset — list them: $\varnothing, \{a\}, \{b\}, \{a,b\}$.

feedback.0: The square bracket on the right at 5 would mean 5 is included — but here it's a round bracket, so 5 is excluded.
feedback.1: The square bracket at $-23$ means it IS included, so $<$ is wrong there.
feedback.3: You swapped which side is open/closed.

feedback.0: Mutual subset containment means they share ALL elements — the opposite of disjoint.
feedback.2: This is the DEFINITION of set equality — nothing here implies emptiness.

options: No, empty set can't be a subset of a non-empty set
Only if 0 is in the set

feedback.1: By definition, $\varnothing$ is a subset of EVERY set — including non-empty ones. There's nothing in $\varnothing$ to violate the condition.
feedback.2: This has nothing to do with the number 0 being an element.

feedback.0: 3 is hidden inside the inner set $\{3,4\}$, not directly an element of A.
feedback.2: 4 alone is a number, not a set — numbers can't be 'subsets'.
feedback.3: $\{1,2\}$ is not listed as a single element of A; 1 and 2 are separate elements.

modules.title: Venn Diagrams & Universal Set
modules.hook: Think of 'All Instagram followers' as your universal set $U$, with 'people who liked your post' and 'people who commented' as two circles drawn inside it — every operation you'll learn next is just shading regions of this picture.
modules.definition: The **universal set** ($U$) is the 'container' set relevant to a given context — every set under discussion is a subset of $U$. A **Venn diagram** represents $U$ as a rectangle and subsets as circles inside it.

concepts.heading: Choosing a universal set
concepts.body: There's no single 'correct' universal set — it just needs to contain every set being discussed. For triangles, $U$ could be 'all triangles in a plane'. For integers, $U$ could be $\mathbb{Z}$ or $\mathbb{R}$.

diagram.caption: U (rectangle) contains A and B as subsets (circles)

video.caption: Coming soon: reading and shading Venn diagrams

tricks: If a question gives you several sets and asks for a universal set, pick the smallest set that contains ALL of them as subsets.
In diagrams, always draw U as the rectangle, subsets as circles inside it — never the reverse.

examples.question: $A=\{1,3,5\}$, $B=\{2,4,6\}$, $C=\{0,2,4,6,8\}$. Suggest a universal set.
steps: Need a set containing 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,8 at minimum
examples.answer: $U = \{0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8\}$ works (any superset of this also works).

quiz.q: Which shape represents the universal set in a Venn diagram?

feedback.0: Circles represent subsets INSIDE U, not U itself.
feedback.2: Triangles aren't used in standard Venn diagram convention.

quiz.q: For the 'set of all right triangles', a suitable universal set is:
options: The set of all right triangles
The set of all triangles in a plane

feedback.0: U must be BROADER than the set itself to be useful — this is circular.
feedback.2: Rectangles aren't triangles at all — wrong category.

quiz.q: $A=\{1,3,5\}, B=\{2,4,6\}$. Which CANNOT serve as a universal set for both?

feedback.0: This contains every element of A and B — valid.
feedback.1: A superset with extra elements still works fine.
feedback.3: All natural numbers certainly contains A and B.

quiz.q: In a Venn diagram, where are elements NOT belonging to any subset placed?
options: Inside the rectangle but outside all circles

feedback.0: Everything relevant to the context sits inside U (the rectangle) — even elements not in any subset.
feedback.3: The intersection is for elements common to circles, not outsiders.

modules.title: Union, Intersection & Difference
modules.hook: People who liked OR commented on your post ($\cup$), people who did BOTH ($\cap$), and people who liked but didn't comment ($-$) — this trio is the highest-weightage part of the chapter.
modules.definition: **Union** $A \cup B = \{x : x \in A \text{ or } x \in B\}$ — everything in either set. **Intersection** $A \cap B = \{x : x \in A \text{ and } x \in B\}$ — only what's common. **Difference** $A - B = \{x : x \in A, x \notin B\}$ — in A, but remove anything also in B.

concepts.heading: Key properties
concepts.body:
$A \cup B = B \cup A$ and $A \cap B = B \cap A$ (commutative)
$A \cup \varnothing = A$, $A \cap \varnothing = \varnothing$
$A \cup A = A$, $A \cap A = A$
$A \cap (B \cup C) = (A\cap B) \cup (A \cap C)$ (distributive)

tricks: $A - B \ne B - A$ in general — subtraction of sets is NOT commutative, just like number subtraction.
If $B \subset A$, then $A \cup B = A$ and $A \cap B = B$ — shortcut worth memorizing.
$A-B$, $A\cap B$, $B-A$ are always mutually disjoint pieces that together make up $A \cup B$.

examples.question: $A=\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, $B=\{2,4,6,8\}$. Find $A-B$ and $B-A$.
steps: $A-B$: keep A's elements not in B → 1,3,5
$B-A$: keep B's elements not in A → 8

examples.question: $A=\{2,4,6,8\}$, $B=\{6,8,10,12\}$. Find $A \cup B$ and $A \cap B$.
steps: Union: all elements, common ones written once
Intersection: only 6 and 8 appear in both

feedback.0: That's the UNION, not intersection — intersection keeps only common elements.
feedback.2: Those are elements in A but not B — that's $A-B$, not $A\cap B$.
feedback.3: They do share elements (3 and 4) — not disjoint.

feedback.0: That's the intersection — common elements, not what's exclusive to A.
feedback.2: Those elements belong to B, not A — that would be $B-A$.

quiz.q: If $A \cap B = \varnothing$, the sets are called:

feedback.0: Equal sets share ALL elements — the opposite situation.
feedback.2: Universal set is a separate concept (the container set), unrelated to this.

feedback.0: Since every element of B is already in A, the OVERLAP is exactly B, not the larger set A.
feedback.2: They definitely share elements — all of B's elements are shared.
feedback.3: That's the union, which would equal A in this case, but the question asks for intersection.

feedback.1: You intersected A with B, not A with C — check which sets the question asks about.
feedback.2: Those elements are in C but not A — not the intersection.
feedback.3: They do share the element 11.

quiz.q: $A \cup (B \cap C)$ vs $(A \cup B) \cap (A \cup C)$ — what does distributive law say?

feedback.0: The distributive law guarantees these ARE always equal — it's a standard identity.
feedback.2: This identity holds for ALL sets A, B, C — no special condition needed.

feedback.0: Both contain 4 — they overlap, so not disjoint.
feedback.3: $\{6,10\}$ is entirely inside the first set — heavy overlap, not disjoint.

modules.title: Complement & De Morgan's Laws
modules.hook: Everyone in your class MINUS the students present today = the absentees — that's the complement. The single most-flipped formula in board exams is $(A \cup B)' = A' \cap B'$, where students swap $\cup$ and $\cap$ under pressure.
modules.definition: For $A \subset U$, the **complement** $A' = U - A = \{x : x \in U, x \notin A\}$ — everything in the universal set that's NOT in A.

concepts.heading: De Morgan's Laws
concepts.body:
$$(A \cup B)' = A' \cap B'$$$$(A \cap B)' = A' \cup B'$$
In words: complement of a union is the intersection of complements, and complement of an intersection is the union of complements. The operator FLIPS each time.

concepts.heading: Complement laws
concepts.body:
$A \cup A' = U$ (together they make everything)
$A \cap A' = \varnothing$ (nothing is shared)

diagram.caption: A′ — everything in U outside A, shaded

video.caption: Coming soon: De Morgan's Laws — Venn diagram proof

tricks: Memory hook: 'complement flips the operator.' Union becomes intersection and vice versa, every single time.
$A'=U-A$ — if you forget the complement formula, just compute it as a plain set difference from U.

examples.question: $U=\{1,...,10\}$, $A=\{1,3,5,7,9\}$. Find $A'$.
steps: Everything in U not in A

feedback.0: The operator FLIPS under complement — union becomes intersection, not union again.
feedback.2: This drops the complement entirely — wrong law altogether.
feedback.3: That's the complement of intersection, a different identity.

feedback.1: That's A itself, not its complement.
feedback.2: Complement isn't empty here — plenty of elements in U are outside A.
feedback.3: Complement isn't the whole universal set — A does share some elements with U.

feedback.0: Double complement returns you to the ORIGINAL set A, not U.
feedback.1: Double complement gives back A, not the empty set.
feedback.3: That would mean no change happened — but complementing twice cancels out to A.

feedback.0: A and its complement share NOTHING by definition — the empty set, not U.
feedback.1: A and A' have no common elements at all.

feedback.0: The operator must flip — this keeps intersection, which is the un-flipped (wrong) version.
feedback.2: Dropped the complement — this just returns to union of the original sets.
That's the whole universal set U, not the complement.

feedback.0: It contains one thing: the empty set itself.

options: Elements in both A and B
Elements in A but not B
Elements in B but not A

feedback.0: That's intersection.
feedback.2: That's B−A, the reverse.

feedback.0: Operator must flip to intersection under complement.

questions.q: Number of subsets of a 4-element set:
$\varnothing \subset A$ for any set A is:

feedback.1: It's true for every set A, empty or not.

questions.q: Which is NOT a set (not well-defined)?

feedback.2: 'Tallest' without a cutoff is subjective/ambiguous — not well-defined.

feedback.0: Round bracket at 7 means 7 is excluded.
feedback.2: Square bracket at 3 means 3 IS included, not excluded.

feedback.0: No overlap between a set and its complement — result is empty, not U.

feedback.1: Union should be the LARGER set, which is A here.

feedback.0: Union being empty would mean both sets are empty — too strong a condition.
